data.title: Perpetua Resources' $2.7B Export-Import Bank loan advanced to congressional notice
data.description:
Perpetua Resources (PPTA) up 7.6% in Tuesday's trading after saying the board of the Export-Import Bank of the U.S. agreed to notify Congress of a proposed $2.7B senior secured long-term loan for the development of its stibnite gold-antimony project in Idaho.

The move triggers a 25-day notice period to Congress and is the last formal step before the Ex-Im board will vote on final approval, which Perpetua (PPTA) said it expects soon after the notice period ends.

If the proposed loan is approved, Perpetua (PPTA) said it would have sufficient capital, together with $714M of cash on hand at year-end, to finance the estimated $2.57B capital cost to build the project.

In total, the company would receive a loan of $2.2B, after excluding capitalized interest and fees.

Perpetua (PPTA) also published updated project economics reflecting current commodity prices as well as year-end 2025 capital and operating cost estimates; assuming a $4,500/oz gold price, the updated model reports the project's unlevered, after-tax project net present value of $6.1B and after-tax internal rate of return of 32.3%, reaffirming the stibnite mine as a premier gold-antimony project.

data.title: Taco Bell is letting rewards members decide on a new menu item
data.description:
Taco Bell is asking U.S. customers to choose which international menu item will make its domestic debut through the company's first "Global Taco Vote."

Starting March 26, U.S. Rewards Members can vote daily in the Taco Bell app to decide between the Kickin' Chicken Taco from Thailand and the Butter Chicken Taco from India. The winning item will be available at U.S. locations for a limited time later this year.

The Kickin' Chicken Taco from Thailand features crispy chicken strips, lettuce, a three-cheese blend, pico de gallo, and a spicy sauce wrapped in a flour tortilla. It originally launched in 2019 to coincide with the brand's entry into the Thai market. Meanwhile, the butter chicken taco from India consists of slow-roasted chicken in a tomato cream-based sauce topped with onions, cilantro, lettuce, and cheddar cheese in a fried chalupa shell. The menu item was added in 2023 as part of a menu highlighting Indian culinary heritage.

"This vote is all about celebrating the creativity of Taco Bell menus around the world while bringing globally inspired flavors and innovations to U.S. fans," Amy Ellis Durini, chief marketing and strategy officer, Taco Bell International, said in the press release. "Both tacos have already built passionate followings in their home countries, and now we're giving U.S. fans the power to choose which one earns a spot on menus nationwide."

The voting period runs through April 1, and the winning menu option will be announced on April 2.

Taco Bell was spun off from PepsiCo (PEP) in 1997,  along with KFC and Pizza Hut, to form a new company, Tricon Global Restaurants, which evolved into Yum! Brands (YUM).

data.title: MiNK outlines initial ARDS Phase II data in H2 2026 while extending runway through 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: MiNK Therapeutics (INKT) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Dr. Jennifer Buell (President, CEO & Director) reiterated MiNK’s differentiation as “an off the shelf” allogeneic iNKT platform that is “administered without lymphodepletion, without HLA matching,” and said the company has “demonstrated clinical activity with a very favorable safety profile.”
  • Dr. Buell (President, CEO & Director) highlighted oncology durability disclosed from the solid tumor program, citing “median overall survival exceeding 23 months” in combination with PD-1 therapies and “complete remissions extending beyond 2 years,” with the focus on checkpoint-refractory settings.
  • Dr. Buell (President, CEO & Director) framed ARDS/hypoxemic pneumonia as the most near-term randomized catalyst, describing prior critically ill dosing experience and stating, “As a result of these findings, we are now well on our way to announcing the first dosing of patients in our randomized Phase II study that's designed to expand to a Phase II/III study,” with sites “planned to launch with our colleagues at top centers in Ukraine and in the U.S.”
  • Dr. Buell (President, CEO & Director) emphasized non-dilutive funding and partnerships as a core operating model, including for the GvHD effort: “We have secured external funding to advance MiNK cells into graft-versus-host disease with the trial in the activation phase at University of Wisconsin.”
  • Melissa Orilall (Principal Financial Officer) said, “During 2025, we executed an at-the-market facility in a disciplined manner, ending the year with a cash balance of $13.4 million,” and added, “Since year-end, we have raised an additional $3 million through this program, extending our runway through 2026 and supporting key clinical milestones.”

Outlook

  • Dr. Buell (President, CEO & Director) guided to near-term clinical milestones rather than numerical financial guidance, saying ARDS/hypoxemic pneumonia dosing is “starting imminently” and that “we expect an initial clinical data in the second half of this year,” alongside initial data from GvHD “in the second half of the year.”
  • Dr. Buell (President, CEO & Director) also pointed investors to multiple near-term updates: “in the first half of 2026… multiple data presentations and peer-reviewed publications,” and for MiNK-215, “we would expect to be announcing some within the next 3 to 5 months on our 215 program.”
  • Versus the prior quarter, management language shifted from a planned pulmonary launch “within weeks” to emphasizing imminent dosing and a defined timing for “initial clinical data in the second half of this year,” while continuing to position the pulmonary program as potentially expandable from Phase II to Phase II/III.

Financial Results

  • Melissa Orilall (Principal Financial Officer) reported a Q4 2025 net loss of $2.6 million, or $0.56 per share, and a full-year 2025 net loss of $12.5 million, or $2.93 per share.
  • Melissa Orilall (Principal Financial Officer) tied spending to pipeline execution, stating the results “reflect continued focused investment in advance in our agenT-797 clinical programs… while maintaining disciplined control over our operational spend.”
  • Dr. Buell (President, CEO & Director) characterized year-over-year cost and balance sheet changes, stating, “our cash increasing to about $13.4 million from $4.6 million and our operating cost decreasing nearly 40% over the course of the year.”

Q&A

  • Emily Bodnar, H.C. Wainwright: Asked about ARDS Phase II size and control arm and how IPF fits; Dr. Buell (President, CEO & Director) said the control context is “standard of care” and “physicians' choice,” adding, “The cells will be added on top of standard of care,” while emphasizing MiNK iNKT activity “independent of steroids being on board” and that the program is led by “Dr. Terese Hammond.”
  • Emily Bodnar, H.C. Wainwright: Asked timing for second-line gastric cancer efficacy data; Dr. Buell (President, CEO & Director) replied, “You will be seeing some efficacy data in the first half of this year at a major conference,” and said IPF planning updates are coming: “You're going to be hearing more about the design of that trial and the development path as we advance over the next couple of months.”
  • Mayank Mamtani, B. Riley: Asked about ARDS patient definition and differentiation, plus overlap with an IL-15 iNKT combination study appearing on clinicaltrials.gov; Dr. Buell (President, CEO & Director) said ARDS enrollment will follow “a very specific global ARDS definition system,” with selection “based on their oxygenation,” and characterized current ARDS standard as “physician's choice,” while describing lung sampling and immune monitoring approaches.
  • Mayank Mamtani, B. Riley: Pressed on the IL-15 combination posting; Dr. Buell (President, CEO & Director) responded, “I want to be very clear that MiNK has not formally announced any collaboration or any clinical trials with the IL-15 superagonist,” and added, “The study that I've referenced is the MiNK-sponsored randomized Phase II trial that we have not yet posted on clinicaltrials.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral-to-slightly negative, with repeated pressure on trial design specifics and external disclosures, particularly around the ARDS control arm and the IL-15 combination posting on clinicaltrials.gov.
  • Management tone was slightly positive and clarifying, leaning on confidence in execution timing and defensive clarity on partnerships, including, “I want to be very clear that MiNK has not formally announced any collaboration.”
  • Compared with the prior quarter, management maintained an execution-focused tone, but the current call included more direct clarification around third-party trial listings and collaboration status, prompted by investor/analyst scrutiny.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management continued emphasizing durability in refractory solid tumors and the pulmonary program as the next major value driver, but Q4 placed more emphasis on imminent randomized ARDS dosing, a global footprint (Ukraine and U.S.), and a timed expectation for “initial clinical data in the second half of this year.”
  • Financial leadership changed quarter-over-quarter: Q3 featured Christine Klaskin (Treasurer and Principal Financial & Accounting Officer), while Q4 introduced Melissa Orilall as newly appointed Principal Financial Officer.
  • Capital strategy messaging shifted from Q3’s cash balance of $14.3 million at quarter-end to Q4’s year-end cash of $13.4 million plus “an additional $3 million” raised since year-end, with continued emphasis on extending runway “through 2026.”

Risks and Concerns

  • Dr. Buell (President, CEO & Director) underscored ARDS clinical and operational complexity, describing severely ill patients and ICU settings, while positioning practicality as mitigation: “We can get the cells into patients in community hospitals.”
  • Partnership and disclosure risk surfaced in Q&A, where management addressed market confusion directly: “MiNK has not formally announced any collaboration” related to the IL-15 superagonist.
  • Funding discipline remained a stated constraint for expansion into IPF, with Dr. Buell (President, CEO & Director) saying MiNK will be “very responsible… about how we're going to be funding that program.”

Final Takeaway

MiNK management centered the 2026 investment narrative on near-term randomized execution in ARDS/hypoxemic pneumonia, with first dosing described as imminent and “initial clinical data” expected in the second half of the year, alongside GvHD trial activation and dosing supported by non-dilutive funding. Management also highlighted updated durability signals in checkpoint-refractory solid tumors and positioned capital efficiency as core, citing year-end cash of $13.4 million, an additional $3 million raised since year-end, and runway “through 2026,” while using the Q&A to explicitly clarify that MiNK “has not formally announced any collaboration” tied to an IL-15 superagonist trial listing.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: S&P 500 stocks with biggest Y/Y jump in fund ownership breadth - BofA
data.description:
Artificial intelligence-related stocks (AIEQ) are dominating the list of S&P 500 (SP500) (SPY) (VOO) names seeing the biggest increase in fund ownership breadth over the past year, according to a new Bank of America Research screen.

Palantir Technologies (PLTR) experienced the most dramatic jump, with fund ownership climbing to 32% from just 8% a year ago. The data intelligence company has emerged as a clear favorite among institutional investors expanding their AI exposure.

The screen also highlighted GE Vernova (GEV), Amphenol (APH), Western Digital (WDC), Broadcom (AVGO), AMD (AMD) and Arista Networks (ANET) alongside Meta (META) as names attracting broader fund participation. Notably, more managers are adding these stocks to their portfolios rather than simply increasing existing position sizes. Gilead Sciences (GLD) and McKesson (MCK) were also in the mix.

The composition of the list reveals how the AI investment thesis has evolved beyond semiconductor demand. Fund managers are now targeting companies across power equipment, data infrastructure, connectivity and enterprise software as direct beneficiaries of the technology shift.

This expanding roster of AI-adjacent plays helps explain why the trade has maintained momentum even as valuations in some corners of the technology sector have grown difficult to justify. For institutional investors, the opportunity set continues to widen as AI applications reach deeper into the economy.

data.title: Oracle begins laying off employees to support AI buildout: report
data.description:
Oracle (ORCL) started laying off employees on Tuesday to drive down costs to support its artificial intelligence infrastructure buildout, according to Business Insider.

The number of employees being laid off is publicly unknown, but it appears to affect Oracle workers around the globe, the report said.

Seeking Alpha reached out to Oracle, but a spokesperson declined to comment on the matter. 

Oracle sent out an email on Tuesday, which was obtained by Business Insider, to its affected employees.

"We are sharing some difficult news regarding your position," the email read. "After careful consideration of Oracle's current business needs, we have made the decision to eliminate your role as part of a broader organizational change. As a result, today is your last working day."

Oracle had approximately 162,000 full-time employees as of May 31, 2025, according to its most recent 10-K filing. About 58,000 were employed in the U.S., and 104,000 were employed internationally. On a segment basis, Oracle had 29,000 in cloud services and license support operations; 31,000 in sales and marketing; 3,000 in hardware; 37,000 in services; 50,000 in research and development; and 12,000 in general and administrative.

Many of these are long-term staffers, as the average tenure with the company at the time was eight years, with 30% of its workforce being there a decade or more.

Tech Layoff Tracker posted on X that the layoffs will affect 18%, or 30,000, of Oracle's global workforce.

During its third-quarter fiscal 2026 earnings report earlier this month, Oracle said it expects capital expenditures of $50B for the entirety of fiscal 2026. The company also expects its fiscal 2027 revenue to hit $90B. Oracle's share price has plummeted more than 50% since striking an all-time high of $328 in September 2025.

Oracle is also involved in a deal to take control of TikTok's (TIKTOK) U.S. operations from Chinese parent ByteDance (BDNCE) to a new American entity. The Larry Ellison-founded company is also a central figure in the ambitious Stargate Project.

data.title: Utilities is the most shorted sector in March
data.description:
Utilities (XLU) is the most shorted sector among the S&P’s 11.

The current short interest as a percentage of float vs. three months ago for utilities is almost at 3%, followed by materials (XLB) at about 2.7% and real estate (XLRE) at about 2.6%.

 

 

 

Moreover, when looking at active managers, long-only holdings remain the most underweight in interest rate-sensitive sectors, such as real estate (XLRE) and utilities (XLU). However, ownership is slightly elevated, compared to history, at more than 1 standard deviation above the historical average.

Utilities (XLU) saw the lowest long-only exposure by active managers, as of the end of March, at -0.05 in relative weight change.

In addition, real estate (XLRE) and utilities (XLU) have the most negative betas—volatility and risk—to rising interest rates.

Utilities ETFs: (XLU), (VPU), (FUTY), (IDU), (FXU), and (JXI).

data.title: T1 Energy targets April close for remaining $350M G2_Austin Phase 1 funding while maintaining 3.1-4.2 GW 2026 G1 output
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: T1 Energy Inc. (TE) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Daniel Barcelo framed the strategy around completing domestic vertical integration, saying, "In 2026, we are building our G2_Austin solar cell fab to complete our vertically integrated domestic solar chain in the U.S. market that completely changed on January 1 with the implementation of new federal rules on foreign content and ownership." He added, "Next year, 2027 is the year we intend to deliver a step-change in our ability to generate earnings and cash flow."

  • Barcelo highlighted Q4 capital formation and commercial steps tied to G2, including "a $72 million registered direct common equity offering," "a $50 million convertible preferred tranche" from Encompass-managed funds, and a December raise "raising combined gross proceeds of $322 million" through concurrent common equity and convertible notes. He said Phase 1 construction at G2 is underway and reiterated the Phase 1 production goal: "Following the completion of Phase 1, we expect to begin producing high efficiency, high domestic content solar cells by the end of this year with an annual capacity of 2.1 gigawatts."

  • Barcelo also pointed to the Treaty Oak Clean Energy deal timing and volume, stating it was "highlighted by a 3-year agreement for T1 to supply 900 megawatts of G1 modules with G2 domestic cells starting in 2027." On the existing Dallas module plant, he said, "quarterly production and sales surpassed 1 gigawatt for the first time" in Q4.

  • CFO Evan Calio described liquidity and funding capacity, saying, "T1 ended 2025 with a much improved liquidity position in a fully ramped factory that hit our production targets" and "we're able to raise more than $440 million in the fourth quarter." Calio said one-time items affected 2025 EBITDA and "account for much of the miss versus guidance," citing several specific drivers.

  • SVP Otto Erster Bergesen said G2 construction and engineering are advancing, and noted the remaining build requirement: "the remaining CapEx required to complete Phase 1, which now stands at $350 million." He added, "we're currently at 90% design" and said T1 expects equipment to arrive "over the summer."

Outlook

  • Management maintained G1_Dallas 2026 production and sales targets, with Barcelo stating, "We are maintaining our production and sales targets of 3.1 to 4.2 gigawatts for G1 in 2026, and we are growing increasingly comfortable with our ability to achieve the high end of that to target range."

  • Calio flagged near-term variables that could shift pricing and timing, including "a ruling in a Section 232 case" and "customer safe harboring activity and projected timelines." He also said the company expects timing shifts between quarters: "we're deferring some 1Q deliveries and expect a significant shift in sales volumes from 1Q to 2Q 26 due to customer requests and timelines," adding, "The shift does not change our expected 2026 revenue or adjusted EBITDA, only the timing."

  • Compared with Q3, the company’s messaging moved from preparing to start G2 construction to stating active build progress and a specific financing timeline. Barcelo said, "we remain in advanced discussions on both fronts and expect to close funding in April," referring to offtake and financing for Phase 1.

Financial Results

  • Calio attributed 2025 EBITDA underperformance primarily to nonrecurring items, saying, "The nonrecurring and unusual items included" an "accounting classification of $34 million sales commission waiver," "Net sales were $16 million lower than expected" tied to year-end regulatory restrictions and 45x retention, "Net sales were $22.7 million lower due to customer offtake true-up," and "we incurred $15 million" of higher-than-forecast tariffs on imported cells.

  • On operating scale at G1_Dallas, Barcelo said, "we produced a total of 2.79 gigawatts of solar modules in 2025, meeting our annual production target," and described a cost and margin improvement path for 2026, stating, "we anticipate that sales and EBITDA will improve each quarter through year-end" based on delivery schedules and "reduced overall costs."

  • No EPS or quarterly revenue figures were explicitly provided in the Q4 2025 transcript, so comparisons to analyst estimates were not included.

Q&A

  • Philip Shen, ROTH Capital Partners: asked for color on the remaining Phase 1 funding and April timing; CEO Daniel Barcelo responded, "We are confident that it will be in April" and said the company "have passed on certain... higher-cost options" while staying on schedule; CFO Evan Calio added T1 wants flexibility to expand G2 and wants to "maximize... our merchant exposure as we move into the year."

  • Philip Shen, ROTH Capital Partners: asked to name new large customers and discuss pipeline cadence; CEO Barcelo said, "Treaty Oak did allow for public disclosure of their name. The others prefer confidentiality" and added, "We remain close on a significant contract."

  • Philip Shen, ROTH Capital Partners: asked about monetizing European assets; CEO Barcelo said T1 has "hired [ Pareto ] to start marketing" the Nordic sites and described market pricing as "anywhere from $0.5 million a megawatt to $1 million in megawatt" for power in the Nordic market.

  • Gregory Lewis, BTIG: asked about the shift in IP to Evervault and potential India AD/CVD implications; CEO Barcelo said, "Evervault is a Singaporean entity, and we are licensing from Evervault" and added, "we don't have operations in India"; Chief Legal & Policy Officer Andy Munro said, "what we have with Evervault is simply an IP license" and called it "only reducing the risk that we face going forward on the [ FIOC ] front."

  • Sean Milligan, Needham: asked about reductions to Trina-related service and sales agreements; CFO Calio responded, "we are moving away from service agreements with Trina" and said the changes "saved an estimated $30 million to $100 million at a 3 to 5 gigawatt run rate," later confirming the reduction would be reflected in SG&A.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was neutral to slightly skeptical, concentrated on financing certainty for G2, customer identity/pipeline conversion, compliance/IP structure, and the magnitude/location of cost reductions.

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more cautious in Q&A on disclosure, with Barcelo stating, "We just can't give too much color" on financing sources and reiterating, "We're committing to April."

  • Versus Q3, management language shifted from readiness and planning (breaking ground “before year-end”) to execution and near-term commitments (April financial close; construction milestones; explicit one-time EBITDA headwinds), while analyst questioning remained focused on compliance, contracts, and funding visibility.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The G2 narrative progressed from Q3’s plan to "start construction... before year-end" to Q4 reporting Phase 1 is "progressing on schedule" and targeting "full financial close of the remaining $350 million... in April."

  • Guidance framing changed from Q3 emphasis on 2025 EBITDA guidance ($25 million to $50 million) and integrated run-rate EBITDA targets to Q4 emphasis on bridge-year execution, supplier qualification, and quarter-to-quarter timing shifts, with Calio stating timing changes "does not change our expected 2026 revenue or adjusted EBITDA."

  • Q4 included a more detailed explanation of why results diverged from prior expectations, with Calio listing several nonrecurring drivers behind "the miss versus guidance," which was not similarly itemized in the Q3 call.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited regulatory and trade-policy uncertainty as swing factors, including "a potential Section 232 ruling" and ongoing adjustment around "customer safe harboring activity" and "new supply chain restrictions."

  • The company identified near-term execution dependencies for G2, including the need to secure Phase 1 funding and offtake, with Barcelo saying, "our sales and finance teams have been busy working to secure an additional offtake contract and to line up capital formation options" and expecting to close funding in April.

  • Management described 2025 volatility and cost impacts tied to policy changes and tariffs, including "higher-than-forecasted tariffs on imported" inputs and forced year-end actions to retain 45x eligibility.

Final Takeaway

T1’s Q4 call centered on executing the “bridge year” to a domestic-cell step change: management reiterated 2026 G1 production and sales targets of 3.1 to 4.2 gigawatts, described G2_Austin Phase 1 construction as on schedule with 2.1 gigawatts of annual capacity planned, and targeted an April financial close for the remaining $350 million needed for Phase 1. Management also pointed to specific Q4 commercial progress (including the 900-megawatt Treaty Oak agreement starting in 2027) and detailed nonrecurring 2025 headwinds—spanning a $34 million accounting treatment issue, net sales shortfalls tied to year-end regulatory shifts and true-ups, and $15 million in higher tariffs—while emphasizing a cost and profitability improvement path as Trina-related agreements are reduced and G1 operations mature.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Accenture remains top pick at UBS on valuation discount
data.description:
• Accenture (ACN) is still a top pick at UBS in the business, education, and professional services sector, as the technology consulting firm is trading at a discount to the S&P 500 for the first time in more than 15 years.
  • “With Accenture stock currently trading at a discount to the S&P 500 for the first time in 15+ years with ~12.8x FY2 P/E vs. our target multiple [of] 19.5x, we believe the market is undervaluing ACN’s earnings growth amid macro uncertainty,” analysts at the firm wrote in a note to clients. “Despite bookings execution and FCF acceleration, we see current sentiment-driven dislocation an opportunity as we remain constructive with [a] $320 PT.”

data.title: Tax Foundation: Liberation Day taxes did not make the U.S. economy better
data.description:
A year after U.S. President Trump declared April 2 “Liberation Day” and promised his tariffs would mark the day “American industry was reborn,” none of the administration’s central economic claims have materialized, experts from Tax Foundation said in a recent article.

The tariff regime, which at its peak reached the highest rates since 1911, was struck down by the Supreme Court in February 2026 when justices ruled the authority used to impose them was not authorized.

While Trump claimed up to $18T in new foreign investment would flow into the U.S. and that “jobs and factories will come roaring back,” the data tells a starkly different story, Tax Foundation experts said.

According to their report, actual foreign direct investment totaled just $288.4B in 2025—below the prior 10-year average of $320.7B—and manufacturing employment declined by 89,000 jobs between April 2025 and February 2026, continuing a pre-existing downward trend.

Tax Foundation experts noted that while tariffs generated $264B in customs duties in 2025, this fell far short of the president’s prediction that they would “bring in trillions and trillions of dollars to pay down America’s debt.”

The research shows that net revenue was further diminished because tariffs mechanically reduced income and payroll tax collections, and the federal debt continued to grow throughout the administration.

Contrary to claims that domestic production would deliver “lower prices for consumers,” research from Harvard’s Pricing Lab estimates a 24% tariff pass-through to retail prices, contributing 0.76 percentage points to Consumer Price Index inflation.

Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell attributed much of the remaining inflation to tariffs, explaining that “these elevated readings largely reflect inflation in the goods sector, which has been boosted by the effects of tariffs.”

The analysis concludes that “the evidence shows the tariffs were not reciprocal,” explaining that rates were not based on actual foreign trade barriers but on synthetic calculations derived from bilateral goods trade balances.

The Tax Foundation noted that U.S. tariff policy changed more than 50 times in a single year, creating volatility and uncertainty that weighed heavily on investment and hiring decisions throughout the economy.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

More on the U.S. economy

  • Job openings continue to dwindle in February: JOLTS report
  • Consumer confidence unexpectedly strengthens in March
  • Chicago PMI falls more than expected in March
  • Home-price growth decelerates in January: S&P Cotality Case-Shiller Home Price Index
  • FHFA house price index rises modestly in January

data.title: Infographic: McCormick and Unilever Foods Combine to Create $20B Global Flavor Powerhouse
